title: Could Scotland Ban US Military Planes from Prestwick Airport?

sections.heading: What the BBC found
sections.content: First Minister John Swinney is seeking a meeting with the UK government over American military use of Prestwick Airport after BBC reporting linked some stopovers at the Scottish government-owned airport to the US-Israeli air campaign against Iran. The Ministry of Defence said Prestwick was not being used to launch strikes and said the UK had authorised the United States to use British military bases for specific defensive missions to protect the UK and its partners.

sections.heading: Aircraft movements under scrutiny
sections.content: Using publicly available flight logs, BBC Scotland said three Boeing KC-135 Stratotankers stopped at Prestwick on 17 and 18 February after leaving Riverside Air Force Base in California. The aircraft then flew on to Saudi Arabia before deploying to Israel. Defence analyst Jonathan Marcus said such tankers are crucial because they extend the range and endurance of strike aircraft and are likely to be helping sustain both US and Israeli operations.

sections.heading: Why Prestwick matters
sections.content: Prestwick Airport was bought by the Scottish government for £1 in 2013 and reported a £3.5m profit in the 2024/25 financial year, with refuelling services its biggest source of revenue. US government spending records cited by the BBC showed the Department of Defense spent £15.3m at Prestwick in 2025 and nearly £105m there since 2018. The Scottish government said it had asked the UK government for clarity on whether any aircraft had passed through Scottish government-owned airports as part of offensive operations.

sourceReferences.citationText: serper-scottish-aviation – Could Scotland ban US military planes from Prestwick Airport?
